infons.title: Keywords

passages.text: Machine learning approaches enable the discovery of therapeutics across domains

Multi-modal datasets have grown exponentially in the last decade. This has created an enormous demand for machine learning models that can predict complex outcomes by leveraging cellular, molecular, and humoral profiles. Corresponding inference of mechanisms can help to uncover new therapeutic targets. Here, we discuss how biological principles guide the design of predictive models and how interpretable machine learning can lead to novel mechanistic insights. We provide descriptions of multiple learning techniques and how suited they are to domain adaptations. Finally, we talk about broad learning capabilities of foundation models on large datasets and whether they can be used to provide meaningful inference about biological datasets.
Graphical abstract

Das and Chhibbar discuss how biological principles guide the design of predictive and interpretable machine learning models. The authors provide descriptions of multiple learning techniques and how they can be used to design novel therapies. They also discuss broad learning capabilities of foundation models on large datasets for meaningful inference.
Introduction

Next-generation sequencing has revolutionized molecular biology by enabling the rapid sequencing of DNA, RNA, and proteins at different resolutions. This technology allows for the parallel sequencing of millions of DNA fragments, providing comprehensive insights into genomics, epigenomics, transcriptomics and proteomics. These advances have been applied to understand the molecular variations present across populations and individuals to precisely design therapeutic interventions. These include understanding the genetics associated with variable efficacy of drugs like warfarin and designing therapies by studying immunogenicity of unique sets of genetic alterations that arise in cancer patient’s tumor cells (neo-antigens). However, conventional statistical approaches used for deconvolving information do not account for differences in data modalities, highly correlated variables in datasets, and large numbers of irrelevant features, which make the analyses and integration of high-dimensional -omic datasets challenging.
Data modalities for predictive machine learning
Schematic explaining the end-to-end predictive modeling using machine learning models on different data modalities.

Various machine learning and deep learning algorithms have been developed especially for high-dimensional datasets with increasing applications in molecular biology. The design of precise molecular therapies relies on this information to find specific cellular targets. These considerations have motivated the aggregation of cellular profiles from different studies to form global atlases such as the human cell atlas. Coupled with these, advanced machine learning methods have been developed to learn multi-modal and, in some cases, dynamic relationships from these datasets to specifically target relevant biological systems. These approaches include scalable and fast annotation of different cell types, deconvolution of single-cell profiles from bulk datasets, and predicting the drug-induced changes to gene expression. In addition, in silico methods have been used to prioritize targets for different molecular therapies across data modalities. The success of these computational methods depends on usability, scalability, and adaptability to the specific biological context. The successful applications of computational learning systems require a sufficient understanding of their nuances for their incorporation with biological information to design better therapies. In this review, we highlight different machine learning techniques and concepts, along with their applications in relevant fields. We provide a conceptual breakdown of several algorithms and discuss fundamental questions regarding the explainability of machine learning algorithms, including advantages and limitations (Figure 1).
Building black boxes: Software for biological predictive modeling

The primary function of learning algorithms is to learn the relationship between the observed data and a response variable for making predictions on unlabeled data points. Most deep learning algorithms are black boxes and we do not understand the reasoning behind their prediction decisions. In this section, we highlight several learning methods and how their predictions have been used in multiple contexts.
Artificial neural networks

An artificial neural network (ANN) is the primary deep learning architecture. ANNs mimic the brain in that it is a network of information-processing nodes called neurons. ANNs are multi-layered, where the first layer takes the input, and the final layer gives the result depending on the prediction task. The middle hidden layers process the input and convert them to meaningful numerical representations to learn important patterns in the dataset. ANNs have a weighted connection between nodes, which determines the importance of information processed by a specific neuron. The weights are updated and transformed to minimize the difference between the true and the predicted values. ANNs can be trained on large quantitative datasets to predict a binary outcome (classification) or a continuous outcome (regression). Theoretically, neural networks can approximate different mathematical relationships, and different neural network architectures of varying complexities have become widely popular across domains. They have been applied in various therapeutic contexts but we shall discuss the prediction of peptide and major histocompatibility complexes (MHCs) as an important case study. The MHC binds and presents a protein fragment or peptide to trigger a T cell immune response. The polymorphic nature and structural promiscuity of the binding regions of the human leukocyte antigen (HLA) enables the presentation of a large repertoire of peptides. Characterizing this immunopeptidome has broad applications in vaccine design, cancer immunotherapy, and understanding autoimmune disorders. Notably, cancers have immune evasion strategies that are vulnerable to the recognition of altered peptides by MHC. The discovery of these neo-antigens is the basic principle behind the development of cancer vaccines. NetMHCPan is a web server that uses specific machine learning strategies to predict peptide-MHC interactions. In more detail, NetMHCPan makes these predictions by quantifying critical sequence features such as the presence of certain motifs on the MHC sequence and the sequence composition of the peptide binding core and training an ANN on them. The ability of ANNs to be trained on quantitative target data makes it ideally suited for constructing sequence to affinity models like the peptide-MHC system. Additionally, ANNs can also capture the higher-order correlations, which are important for modeling positional correlations in receptor-ligand interactions. Subsequent algorithmic adaptations of NetMHCPAN have helped to predict MHC-bound peptides across the HLA-allele spectrum.

Another tool for prediction of peptide-MHC interactions is MHCFlurry. MHCFlurry is an open source software package that can predict peptide-MHC interactions using an ensemble of ANN models for each MHC allele. Ensembling is a technique where multiple models are combined to improve the overall predictive performance compared with individual models. The idea is that, by aggregating multiple weak or strong learners, we can reduce variance, bias, or both, leading to better generalization. The subsequent development of the ANN architecture has led to data-dependent adaptations of the model. In the subsequent subsections, we discuss these specialized deep learning models and their applications in molecular therapies.
Recurrent neural networks

Recurrent neural networks (RNNs) are a type of ANN that are designed to handle sequential data and are adept at preserving the importance of inherent order in the input. In a regular ANN, inputs are processed independent of previous inputs. RNNs have a memory for past inputs in a sequence and can also process sequences of different lengths as compared with fixed size inputs for ANNs. RNNs can also recognize that earlier parts of the sequence influence later parts. For example, in a sentence, the meaning of a word often depends on previous words. ANNs treat inputs independently and thus cannot capture such relationships. Due to the aforementioned advantages, RNNs can be used for effectively modeling biological sequences and glean critical insights.

Hill et al. used RNNs for deciphering the RNA protein coding potential by training the model on mRNA transcripts to predict the protein-coding potential of newly identified RNA transcripts. Feature-based methods are limited by current scientific knowledge, but deep learning methods like RNNs can use the RNA sequence alone to figure out important sequence patterns that correlate with protein coding potential. Methods like RNN can be used for harvesting new biological insights from the rising flood of sequencing data. RNNs have also been used for discovering transcription factor (TF) binding sites in the DNA sequence, but we shall talk more about this challenging task in the next section, where ANNs used for capturing information in images were adapted to better understand gene regulation.
Convolutional neural networks

Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) are used extensively for processing grid-like data such as images. They use a multi-layer structure where each layer has a specific function. The namesake convolutional layer uses small filters to detect edges, textures, and patterns. The output of these filters is transformed using specialized functions and the pooling layer reduces the input size while keeping all the important features for faster downstream computation. After all the mentioned processing, a conventional ANN processed the output to make the final predictions. Convolutions can also be used to detect patterns in high-dimensional biological datasets. This has been used to detect TF footprints within regions of accessible chromatin in a cell-type-specific manner. This is important to determine the regulatory determinants of gene expression, as TF motifs are general consensus sequences whose corresponding enrichment does not imply TF binding. TFs will selectively bind a small fraction of potential sites in a highly cell-context-specific manner. ChromBPNet uses a CNN to model unstranded, base-resolution assay for transposase-accessible chromatin sequencing (ATAC-seq) or DNase sequencing cleavage profiles in 1-kb genomic segments as a function of local regulatory DNA sequence context. The predictions are used to prioritize specific TF motifs in genomic sequences. Importantly, context-specific TF binding predictions correlate with genomic localization of disease-associated variants that frequently disrupt TF occupancy and gene expression.
Representation learning

Conventional learning approaches rely on manually designed features for prediction, but this is time consuming and severely limits applications to well-annotated fields. However, we can use neural networks to transform the original data into numerical representations that best capture the most important patterns in images, sequences and other data types without manual feature augmentation. This is known as representation learning.

Although the representations are generated in an unsupervised manner, they can be used as features for training classifiers for different tasks. Sliding window interaction grammar (SWING), a representation learning-based method, uses previous work done to extract semantic latent features from documents to convert a biochemically encoded interaction language associated with biological systems. SWING was used effectively in predicting peptide-MHC interactions and the impact of missense variants on specific protein-protein interactions (PPIs). The latter provides variant effect prediction at a molecular resolution, which can be crucial in devising novel therapeutic strategies. Discovery that a population variant in the membrane protein CCR5 conferred resistance to the HIV-1 by disrupting its interaction with the viral envelope glycoprotein led to the development of Maraviroc as a US Food and Drug Administration-approved treatment for HIV-1 that functions by blocking the interface for this interaction. The structural information required to solve this problem is extremely sparse, but the effective use of representation learning could impute important information from just the sequences.
Deep learning methods for graphs

Biological systems are better understood as an interconnected web of molecular entities exerting different effects in a combinatorial manner. These systems can be mathematically represented using the graph data structure. Graphs are widely used in computer science to model relationships between objects and consist of a set of vertices called nodes and connections between these nodes known as edges. The human protein interactome can be represented as a graph, where different proteins are the nodes and binary interactions between them are the edges in the graph. Since biological graphs represent real-world entities, they are more commonly called biological networks. The network representation of living systems becomes critical in designing therapies to understand the global effects of molecular perturbations and the efficacy of the intervention. As convolutions can capture local information within an image to generate numerical representations to learn important patterns, similar methods can be applied to networks to understand local patterns around a node’s neighborhood and their importance to the overall network. Therefore, representation learning architectures specifically made for networks can generate numerical representations for nodes, edges, and the entire network. These representations can be used for modeling the effect of perturbations on a system, which is critical in understanding disease pathology as demonstrated by Yeh et al., where Perturb-seq screens identified perturbations in PTPN1 and ACTR8 in sensitizing ovarian cancer cells to T cell and natural killer cell cytotoxicity. Knowledge of such perturbations, which help cancer cells to evade immunity, can be used to develop effective cancer immunotherapies. The method GEARS applied network representation learning on cellular perturbation profiles to determine the transcriptional outcomes of the cell after perturbation. GEARS incorporates the gene co-expression network (nodes, genes; edges, correlation in gene expression across samples) and gene ontology network (nodes, gene; edges, relationships determining the molecular, biological, and localized function of the gene) to model the effects of a perturbation relying on key intuitions that genes that share similar expression patterns should likely respond similarly to external perturbations and genes involved in similar pathways should impact the expression of similar genes after perturbation. GEARS generates the aggregated numerical representation for the genes by using graph neural networks (GNNs). As mentioned, GNNs help to iteratively aggregate feature information from a node’s local neighborhoods into a numerical representation. Methods like GEARS can leverage learned information from biological networks to infer multi-gene perturbation outcomes. Furthermore, GEARS can guide the design of new screens by identifying perturbations that maximize information gained and minimize experimental cost. Graph representation learning is also useful in meaningfully integrating different biological networks (co-expression, gene regulatory, PPIs networks, etc.).

The interconnectedness in a network is helpful in visualizing long- and short-range relationships between different entities. There is an inherent geometry within networks that is absent in sequential and grid-like representations of data. It can also be explained as a lack of fixed structure present in languages and images. Understanding and learning these geometric patterns can be beneficial in increasing performance biological network deep learning models. This is known as geometric learning. PINNACLE, which uses geometric learning, is a method for providing cell-type-specific context to protein representations and interactions. The context-aware profiling of PPIs using geometric learning can help in the systematic discovery of differential mutation-rewired PPIs to guide tumor-specific therapeutic interventions. This is crucial in deciphering the mutation-induced changes in PPIs in cancer and dependence of tumor on these changes. This has been demonstrated in the context of BTK inhibitor resistance mutations that disable the kinase activity while promoting physical interactions with other kinases to sustain B cell receptor signaling and promote chronic lymphocytic leukemia.
Contrastive learning

The numerical representations generated by different methods can also be used for precisely differentiating between similar and dissimilar data points. This is by informing the representation generating process to contrast the representations proportionate to the degree of dissimilarity between two data points. This process is known as contrastive learning. This concept has been recently applied in the development of SCimilarity, a human-cell-atlas-level model for scalable search of similar human cells. The method queries tens of millions of cells across hundreds of studies to query for similar cellular states across the human body, providing a tool for generating biological insights from the human cell atlas. SCimilarity uses supervised and unsupervised learning principles to represent the expression profiles of matching cell types closer together in the latent representation space and encourage the model to preserve the variation from the input expression profiles, capturing subtler differences in expression pattern within cells of the same type.
ML Potter and the chamber of secrets: Interpretability and inference for better therapies
Prediction and inference in machine learning models
Schematic explaining prediction using machine learning models and deriving inference from them for different biological contexts.

Biological domain adaptability drives the choice of prediction algorithms, but, in engineering novel therapeutic interventions, machine learning models can provide meaningful inference to prioritize reliable targets. Inference-based interpretable machine learning models are different from black box models, where the reasoning used by the algorithm in making decisions is human understandable (Figure 2). Interpretable models also comply with the biologist-in-loop model, as it confers confidence to a model whose reasoning has been confirmed by a domain expert. There are two major interpretable machine learning techniques used to explain prediction in computational biology applications. Models that are naturally interpretable are known as interpretable-by-design models.
Inherently interpretable models
Linear models are classic examples of machine learning models with built in interpretability and use the following equation to model the map between the observed and the response variable.

Here, Y is the label or value to be predicted and X is the input matrix. W is a vector where each value corresponds with the contribution of different features in X to the prediction of Y. E is the error term or the difference between the true and predicted values. Most linear models are trained toward the objective of reducing E. The structure of a linear function can help to inspect the coefficient weights to ascertain the importance of each feature to the prediction.

Although simple, linear models have been applied to solve complex biological problems. This can be illustrated by studying the model behind CellOracle. CellOracle infers base gene regulatory networks (GRNs), elucidating the broad regulatory relationships between gene and TFs from scATAC-seq datasets. It uses a linear model to confer cell-type or context specificity to the GRN by modeling the expression of a target gene as the sum of the weighted expression of its TFs. The inferred weights measure the amount of shift in the target gene’s expression caused by a shift in the expression of a particular TF. Using chain rule on linear equations, CellOracle enables modeling the effect of a perturbation beyond the immediate neighborhood in the GRN. CellOracle has been very successful across biological contexts specifically in defining the state transition of neutrophils dependent on the perturbation of CEBPA expression in sepsis. However, capturing just the linear signal from high-dimensional datasets does not explain all the variability and causal factors. Significant latent factor interaction discovery and exploration (SLIDE) is a first-in-class statistical machine learning approach that uses a latent factor regression approach to move beyond the identification of correlates and biomarkers to the inference of putative biological mechanisms underlying outcomes of interest from high-dimensional -omic datasets. Additionally, it captures the non-linear structure within the datasets as networks to push explainability beyond linear structure through a series of rigorous statistical guarantees that enable it to identify significant interacting latent factors underlying outcomes. SLIDE has been applied on a range of multi-modal datasets from single cell to spatial to identify mechanisms of disease in autoimmune and infectious disease.
Models with post hoc interpretability

The second class of interpretability in machine learning models is post hoc explanations, which are flexible and typically model agnostic due to their application after the design and training of a prediction model. In the mentioned case of ChromBPNet, a post hoc explainer was used to select genomic features most important to the prediction. Particularly, the explainer used in ChromBPNet is DeepLIFT/DeepSHAP, which estimates the contribution of individual bases in the accessible chromatin peaks to the model’s prediction. DeepLIFT is a method that affords explainability by propagating the error back to individual nodes in the network to every feature in the input to quantify their contribution in the final prediction. Post hoc explanation uncovered a bias in predicting the TF motifs toward the sequence preferences of the particular enzymes used to profile chromatin accessibility.

Another method for post hoc explanations is Shapley values. Shapley values come from game theory and measure the contribution of each feature to the output prediction. This is done by comparing all the possible subset of features and measuring how much each feature changes the model’s output when included or removed. Across all the possible scenarios, the contributions are averaged to assign feature importance.

SCARLink can impute the gene expression from chromatin accessibility using Poisson regression and uses a Shapley value analysis for post hoc explanation to identify cell-type-specific gene enhancers. Importantly, the cell-type enhancers prioritized using SHAP on the SCARLink model were validated by promoter capture Hi-C assay and were also enriched with disease variants and expression quantitative trait loci.
Good therapies need a strong foundation: Are foundation models the next frontier in computational biological discovery?

The above sections discussed inference- and prediction-based machine learning models targeted toward specific biological problems. A popular new class of learning models is being used for pan-context biological discovery, which learn on compendium-level datasets in an unsupervised manner and can be slightly retrained to adapt to multiple supervised predictive tasks. These models are foundation models and use a class of learning known as self-supervised learning. In this section, we define essential vocabulary used in the foundation model space along with domains where these models have become popular. We also discuss how explainable these models and potential caveats to their widespread application.
Self-supervised learning

Self-supervised learning defines a pretext task based on unlabeled inputs to produce descriptive and intelligible representations. For example, in natural language, a common objective for self-supervised learners is to mask a word in the text and predict surrounding words. This objective helps the model to capture relationships among words in the text without the need for any labels. Unlike supervised learning, self-supervised learning can learn from vast amounts of unlabeled data.
Transformers
Transformers and attention
Schematic explaining use of transformer models and modeling of global relationships using attention scores.

Foundation models use the transformer architecture, which are self-supervised learners. Transformers have an encoder-decoder structure, where the encoder maps an input sequence of symbol representations to a sequence of continuous representations. The decoder then generates an output sequence of symbols one element at a time. Simply, the encoder reads the input and extracts meaningful information, and the decoder creates a meaningful response based on the encoded information. At each step, the model is auto regressive, using the previously generated symbols as additional input when generating the next. The transformer does not sequentially read the input but instead examines all positions simultaneously and assigns importance to each position in the sequence relative to the others. This is known as attention and helps to keep track of long-range dependencies in a sequence. Attention mechanisms have become an integral part of modeling complex relationships in biological sequences. Additionally, the training process on broad unlabeled data is known as pretraining and the downstream additional training on labeled data of the model to perform specific tasks is known as fine-tuning (Figure 3).
Nucleotide transformers and DNA foundation models

The ability of transformer-based foundation models to encode information from sequence alone has been applied to nucleotide sequences to perform prediction and inference tasks on genomes. These models include Enformer, nucleotide transformer, DNABert, and HyenaDNA. As a case study for genomic foundation models, Enformer was used to effectively predict the gene expression from the DNA sequence alone through the incorporation of long-range genomic interactions. Enformer used the capability of attention layers to model long-range interactions to confer interpretability to the model. On inspection of the gene attention scores, it was observed that they correlated with histone H3 acetylated at K27. The global interactions caught by the attention layer highlighted not only local promoter regions but also distal enhancers more than 20 kb away. A secondary level of explainability was observed in the significantly higher level of attention paid to the sequences proximal to the TAD boundaries as compared with the controls.
Single-cell transcriptomics foundation models

In the previous example, attention matrices could capture long-range genomic interactions. These metrics can also be used to infer global cell-cell and gene-gene networks from high-dimensional single cell datasets. Geneformer is a single-cell transcriptomics foundation model trained on 30 million single-cell transcriptomics to enable context-specific predictions for different biological networks belonging to data-sparse domains. On the prediction side, Geneformer was fine-tuned to perform various tasks such as prediction of dosage sensitivity for different genes for better interpretation of copy number variants in genetic diagnosis and predicting chromatin dynamics predictions. Geneformer is one of the many single-cell transcriptomics foundation models, which include scGPT, cellFM, and scBERT. On the inference side, Geneformer used the inherent properties of the attention layers and confirmed that in the specific context of aortic endothelial cells, genes paid more attention to TFs than other genes, indicating that the attention layers in a self-supervised manner learned the importance of TFs in dissecting cell identity. These relationships also correlated with the most affected genes when critical TFs were perturbed in congenital heart defects. In a previous section, we discussed the importance of predicting downstream perturbation effects in developing therapeutic interventions and attention heads were also critical in the in silico prediction of cell-state changes based on the perturbation of target genes in cardiomyopathies, where the deletion of gene targets leads the cell representations to shift closure to resemble cardiomyopathic cell representations from healthy cell representations. The candidate targets were experimentally corroborated.
Amino acid sequence transformers and protein foundation models

Foundation models and transformers have enjoyed the greatest success in proteomics. In proteomics, AlphaFold is a widely used transformer for accurately predicting protein structures. AlphaFold predicts the protein structures as a graph inference problem in three-dimensional space, where initial versions utilized multiple sequence alignments of the protein and succeeding versions used the pairformer architecture directly on sequences. The success of AlphaFold can be attributed to the biological considerations in the algorithm design process such as the encouragement to satisfy the peptide bond geometry during fine-tuning by a violation loss term and capturing the evolutionary constraint on structures by using the multiple sequence alignment of the protein as an input. Besides the strength of prediction performance, the model can be explained through ablation, where each block in the network was re-trained while keeping all the other parameters constant. The explanation of the transformer gave insights into the trajectory AlphaFold takes into improving the initial prediction of the protein structure at each block in the model. The resolution of the proteome structurally is a major technological leap in biomedical research, with boundless potential in selecting targets for different therapies and linking the vast amounts of genomic data to structural effects and function. This has been expanded in the next iteration of AlphaFold to confidently predict the structures of broad classes of biomolecular interactions and to predict the effect of millions of coding variants through further fine-tuning.

AlphaFold is not the only foundation model for proteins. The evolutionary scale model (ESM) is a protein language model (pLM) transformer used for structure prediction but uses just the protein sequence instead of a multiple sequence alignment. ESMs can also be fine-tuned for protein associated prediction tasks including variant effect prediction. pLMs were used to evolve human antibodies by suggesting mutations that are evolutionary plausible without using any prior information and the predicted candidates showed thermostability and effective viral neutralization against Ebola as well as severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 pseudoviruses.
Caveats in transformers, attention, and foundation models

Although transformers and foundation models can provide accurate predictions along with reliable inference, there are many caveats that need to be explored before their reliable and widespread application. First, these are highly parameterized complex models that require massive amounts of data for their pre-training. An undertrained transformer will generalize poorly, seriously curtailing its fine-tuning capabilities across various tasks. It is important to ascertain whether simpler models with built-in interpretability trained on the same data can give comparable performance. This has been tested in a few scenarios. Boiarsky et al. evaluated scBERT and found that a simple logistic regression baseline outperforms or performs comparably to scBERT. This finding held even in the few-shot learning setting, where the model had access to a limited number of training examples. Despite the massive amounts of pre-training data, scBERT did not show any significant advantage over logistic regression. Plus, the same study reported that learning of meaningful gene representations was not necessary to perform well in the prediction objective. Similarly, Enformer could impute gene expression from DNA sequence alone and characterize important genomic regions through its attention layers. SCARLink is a simple gene-level linear model also capable of imputing gene expression from chromatin potential without peak inference, albeit this imputation is not done solely from DNA sequence. However, in an interpretable manner, SCARLink also showed that predicted genomic regions could capture chromosome conformation data and were significantly enriched for disease-associated genome-wide significant variants. Although this can be a one-off instance, it underlines the need to better evaluate transformer models across standardized tasks with models of different parameterizations.

A major point is the level of interpretability guaranteed by the attention layers. Attention scores and feature importance are shown to not always correlate and hence are not necessarily reliable tools for identifying which input elements are responsible for an output. In certain contexts, rigorous evaluation settings do not exist, making it difficult to ascertain whether the observed inference is critical to the model’s decision-making process. In single-cell transcriptomics foundation models, the attention weights are used for in -silico perturbation analyses; however, computational benchmarking of perturbation prediction remains challenging and has to depend on experimental validation, which severely restricts the confirmation of most predictions and does not produce a quantitative metric of learning evaluation.
Discussion

Major work in developing computational methods has been done in a targeted fashion to solve very specific and niche biological problems. We have discussed how learning architectures have been applied and evolved for better domain adaptation along with representing biological information in the most intuitive way possible. Explainability and interpretability of machine learning models have found applications in delineating biological systems and enhanced prioritization of targets for various therapies by pinpointing sources of signal in high-dimensional albeit noisy biological datasets. We also touched upon different applications of foundation models and how a single large model can be applied to different problems across a domain. We discussed the importance of probing the amount of attention paid to different positions in the input to correlate known biological priors with the decision-making process of transformers.
Outlook

Despite the major advances in machine learning and biology, this progress does not happen in tandem between them. The computational biology community needs interpreters or a common language that translates the innovations between the fields for effective implementation to solve complex problems while propagating a better understanding of caveats associated with any domain. This is critical, especially since the implementation of transformers in the molecular biology space has been done in a short time since the inception of the architecture. Although a meaningful increase in parameters and incorporation of attention scoring has made it a successful model across fields, biology is an equally convoluted science with a high level of unknown parameters. We have to carefully evaluate new and complex models, as the molecular domains in which they will be applied are equally or more complex. As models and our knowledge evolve, so will the challenges in applying models in biology. Consistent algorithmic innovation driven by a deep understanding of the molecular dynamics that drive health and disease will provide pervasive optimal solutions.
